---
title: "2.3.2 Symmetric and asymmetric encryption"
subtitle: "Data Transmission P64-P74"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
        .reveal .term { color: #cf1f2e; font-weight: 700; }
        .reveal .mark { background: #fff36d; padding: 0 0.08em; }
        .reveal .tight-list li { margin-bottom: 0.24em; }
        .reveal .clean-table table { font-size: 0.76em; line-height: 1.1; }
        .reveal .clean-table th, .reveal .clean-table td { padding: 0.24em 0.32em; vertical-align: top; }
        </style>
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 2 Directory / 返回 Unit 2 目录](../../index.html)

## Symmetric Encryption

### Same key for both operations

<span class="term">Symmetric encryption</span> (对称加密) uses an encryption key; the <span class="mark">same key</span> is used to encrypt and decrypt the encoded message.

First, consider a simple system that uses a <span class="mark">10-digit denary encryption key</span> (this gives 1 × 10¹⁰ possible codes). Suppose our encryption key is:

<p style="text-align: center; font-size: 1.4em; font-family: monospace; letter-spacing: 0.3em;"><span class="mark">4 2 9 1 3 6 2 8 5 6</span></p>

This means every letter in a word is <span class="mark">shifted across the alphabet</span> by +4, +2, +9, +1, +3, +6, +2, +8, +5, +6 places, repeating for the whole message.

## Symmetric Encryption Example

### COMPUTER SCIENCE IS EXCITING

Plaintext (top) and ciphertext (bottom) after applying the key 4 2 9 1 3 6 2 8 5 6:

<p style="text-align: center; font-size: 0.85em; font-family: monospace;">
C O M P U T E R &nbsp; S C I E N C E &nbsp; I S &nbsp; E X C I T I N G<br>
<span class="mark">G Q V Q X Z G Z X &nbsp; I M G W D &nbsp; H O U M C &nbsp; I M V R O J</span>
</p>

To get back to the original message, apply the same decryption key but <span class="mark">in reverse</span>: each letter shifted −4, −2, −9, −1, −3, and so on.

For example: 'G' −4 → 'C', 'Q' −2 → 'O', 'V' −9 → 'M'

## Symmetric Encryption: Key Length

### Modern encryption keys

However, modern computers could <span class="mark">'crack' this encryption key in a matter of seconds</span>. To combat this, we now use <span class="mark">256-bit binary encryption keys</span> that give 2²⁵⁶ (approximately 1.2 × 10⁷⁷) possible combinations.

(Even this may not be enough as we head towards <span class="term">quantum computers</span>.)

## Symmetric Encryption Problem

### Key distribution challenge

The real difficulty is <span class="mark">keeping the encryption key a secret</span> (for example, it needs to be sent in an email or a text message which can be intercepted).

::: {.tight-list}
- The <span class="mark">security issue</span> is always the <span class="mark">main drawback of symmetric encryption</span>
- A single encryption key is required for both sender and recipient
- This problem led to the development of <span class="term">asymmetric encryption</span>
:::

## Asymmetric Encryption

### Two keys: public and private

<span class="term">Asymmetric encryption</span> (非对称加密) was developed to overcome the security problems associated with symmetric encryption. It makes use of two keys:

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="mark">Public key</span> (公钥) — made available to everybody
- <span class="mark">Private key</span> (私钥) — only known to the computer user
:::

Both types of key are needed to encrypt and decrypt messages.

The matching pairs of keys are <span class="mark">mathematically linked</span> but can't be derived from each other.

## Tom & Jane: Step 1 and 2

### Asymmetric encryption in action

Suppose Tom and Jane work for the same company and Tom wishes to send a confidential document to Jane:

**Step 1:** Jane uses an algorithm to <span class="mark">generate a matching pair of keys</span> (private and public) that they must keep stored on their computers.

**Step 2:** Jane now <span class="mark">sends her public key to Tom</span>.

```
Tom                         Jane
                            Public key + Private key
Jane sends Tom              (matching pair)
her public key  ←-----------
```

## Tom & Jane: Step 3 and 4

### Encryption and decryption

**Step 3:** Tom now uses <span class="mark">Jane's public key</span> to encrypt the document he wishes to send to her. He then sends his <span class="mark">encrypted document (ciphertext)</span> back to Jane.

**Step 4:** Jane uses her <span class="mark">matching private key</span> to unlock Tom's document and decrypt it.

::: {.tight-list}
- This works because the <span class="mark">public key used to encrypt</span> and the <span class="mark">private key used to decrypt</span> are a matching pair generated on Jane's computer
- <span class="mark">Jane can't use the public key to decrypt</span> the message — only the matching private key works
:::

## Public Key Sharing

### Multiple senders

Jane can also exchange her public key with <span class="mark">any number of people</span> working in the company, so she is able to receive encrypted messages from everyone:

```
Tom ----→ Public key ---
                         |
Susan ---→ Public key ---→ Jane
                         |   Public key
Mike ----→ Public key ---→ Private key
                         |
Megan ---→ Public key ---
```

All senders encrypt using <span class="mark">Jane's public key</span>; only Jane can decrypt using her <span class="mark">matching private key</span>.

## Two-Way Communication

### Everyone needs their own key pair

If a two-way communication is required between all five workers:
- They <span class="mark">all</span> need to <span class="mark">generate their own matching public and private keys</span>
- All users then need to <span class="mark">swap public keys</span> so they can send encrypted documents between each other
- Each worker will then use their <span class="mark">own private key</span> to decrypt information being sent to them

## Symmetric vs Asymmetric Comparison

### Comparing the two encryption methods

::: {.clean-table}
| Aspect | Symmetric | Asymmetric |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Keys | One (shared) | Two (public/private) |
| Speed | Fast | Slower |
| Key distribution | Main drawback — keeping key secret | Solved — public key shared openly |
| How it works | Same key encrypts and decrypts | Public key encrypts, matching private key decrypts |
| Main use | Bulk data encryption | Secure key exchange, where key distribution is the main issue |
:::

The <span class="mark">key distribution problem</span> is the central difference — asymmetric encryption was developed specifically to solve this.

## Key Points Summary

### Symmetric encryption

::: {.tight-list}
- Uses <span class="mark">one key</span> for both encryption and decryption
- Key must be kept <span class="mark">secret</span> — this is the main drawback
- Modern systems use <span class="mark">256-bit binary keys</span> (2²⁵⁶ possible combinations)
- Example: 10-digit key 4291362856 shifts each letter by successive digits
:::

### Asymmetric encryption

::: {.tight-list}
- Uses <span class="mark">two mathematically linked keys</span> — public (shared) and private (secret)
- Public key encrypts → <span class="mark">matching private key decrypts</span>
- Anyone with the public key can send encrypted messages to the key owner
- For two-way communication, <span class="mark">all parties need their own key pair</span> and must swap public keys
:::

## Classroom Check

### Understand encryption types and applications

A complete answer should include:

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="mark">symmetric encryption: same key encrypts and decrypts</span> — fast, but key distribution is the main security drawback
- <span class="mark">256-bit binary keys</span> give 2²⁵⁶ ≈ 1.2 × 10⁷⁷ possible combinations
- <span class="mark">asymmetric encryption: uses public key (shared) and private key (secret)</span> — mathematically linked but can't be derived from each other
- process: <span class="mark">Jane generates key pair → sends public key to Tom → Tom encrypts with public key → Jane decrypts with private key</span>
- <span class="mark">public key can be shared with any number of people</span>; all can send encrypted messages that only the private key holder can decrypt
- for <span class="mark">two-way communication, all parties need their own key pair</span> and must swap public keys
:::

## Bridge

### Return to the main outline

Return to the Unit 2 directory to review the data transmission topics and encryption examples.

## End

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 2 Directory / 返回 Unit 2 目录](../../index.html)
